  • [ - ] Nico1 - member since 22-Jun-16

    Ronaldo fan boys crying about the most stone wall penalty you'll ever see. "Intent" doesn't matter. Whether or not he could have gotten out of the way doesn't matter. Alvarez got to the ball first. The keeper was a second late to the challenge. If the keeper got the ball first and Alvarez clattered into him, you can guarantee it would be a foul. If you're late to a challenge, don't get any of the ball, and wipe out the guy who did get the ball, then it's a foul. Simple as that, now stop crying.

  • [ - ] Gewoonmoi - member since 29-Dec-21

    Shouldn't be a penalty. They run into 'eachother' and Alvarez shoots the ball before the collision. In instances like that, the keeper ought to always be protected.

  • [ - ] tkr - member since 24-Aug-12

    @Gewoonmoi It was pretty dodgy, what is the keeper supposed to do? Penalties like that often come with red cards because they usually involve the keeper trying to take down the forward to prevent him from getting around to score. I don't think he made an effort to trip Alvarez but the ref must have interpreted that there was some kind of obstruction. Surprised they didn't go for VAR.

    3 years ago
     +1 Reply Report Sign in or join us to rate comments! Sign In || Join Us

  • [ - ] lapa29 - member since 13-Dec-22

    @Gewoonmoi Yes it should, when players collide in the box the person who got a touch inside the box gets the call, Croatias goalie didnt touch the ball and just tacled the Argentinian who was considered on ball because he got a touch inside the box. Like it or not thats how it goes, in inside the box collisions the player who gets the touch on ball is fouled. If the keeper got a touch and Alvarez on the ball he would have got a freekick aswell.

  • [ - ] lapa29 - member since 13-Dec-22

    @Lechiffre10 Youre delusional and dont know the rules. In inside the box collisions where only the other one gets a touch on ball, the person who touched the ball always gets fouled by the rulebook. The keeper didnt touch the ball at all and collided with the attacker. If this was allowed you could just bodyslam attackers every time they get inside the box. In the other hand, if the keeper got a touch on the ball and Alvarez didnt he would have gotten a freekick aswell. Like it or not the penalty was totally justified and the rule is very needed, unfortunately it leads to soft penalties where the keeper fouls without intent of doing so.

    3 years ago
     +4 Reply Report Sign in or join us to rate comments! Sign In || Join Us

  • [ - ] DanInter - member since 23-Feb-17

    I don't understand Dalic today. Netherlands clearly showed last round, how you can score vs this Argentina side, just play route-one, put more people in the box and just cross it. Yet he didn't change anything tactically, they still trying to pass it into the goal. The chances they had this game, also came from crosses. Obviously you can see how tired the Croatian side is, after all this team is 4 years older now than last time, when they survived extra time after extra time. But tactically this still doesn't make any sense..

    3 years ago

  • [ - ] Ewerfekt - member since 16-Oct-18

    @koziol.mutant He probably does, but no place in squad. Sadly he shares position with that madman Perisic. Another problem with Orsic is that he is merchent of space and speed. While our team plays on possesion. He is perfect for sub tho. When he comes fresh late his speed and verticality is God given. If you start him he just wont be explosive as he should be late in game. Perisic is also miles ahead of him defensivly. Some people here in Croatia are calling for starting Perisic as fullback so we have spot for Orsic. That would be tactical suicide in my opinion. Perisic improved immensly his defensive play under Conte in last 4 years. His defensive play these days and in Russia 2018 can't even be compared, That said I never seen him play fullback in defensive line of 4. It's much more defensive position then wingback in line of 5. Another idea is to start Orsic as central striker. However I think we are too deep into tournament to run such dangerous experiments.

    3 years ago

  • [ - ] BeautifulLoser - member since 30-Dec-12

    @Sputnik I figure you are young and don't know much about football history. In 2006, Argentina lost on penalties against Germany, and they started a brawl. In 2010, they got the beating they deserved, and they started a brawl. In 2014, they fouled like criminals, and they got what they deserved. Of course, they complained and whined, as they always do. Absolutely unable to congratulate their opponents. For these people, football is synonymous with war, violence, and fraud. There is no beauty and no sportsmanship in what they do. It's sick and desperate. Pressuring the Italian referee in 1978, handballs against the English and the Nigerians in 1986, spiked water against the Brazilians in 1990: that's how they won and tried to win. It's despicable. Every normal person should shun them.

  • [ - ] Icarax - member since 27-Sep-17

    very tough. there is a looming feeling that argentina cant overcome croatia's tenacity and midfield. however id say argentina has more energy in midfield than brazil, and is also statistically great offensively (scored as many goals overall as brazil but spread over more matches), with messi & alvarez's individual brilliance, and with mac allister and enzo hopefully having some sharp forward passes and shots, it is likely that theyre able to break the deadlock first in this match. whilst croatia is a team with great passing, combinations, with the ambition to control the tempo, this will be tested against argentina who is pretty much the same, just less tenacious defensively. i feel croatia style of play also exploits some of the defensive weaknesses of argentina (crosses, second balls), though no 9 like weghorst. on the other hand argentina very rough, decisive with tackles and pressures, croatia may not play the same game as before. 1-1 and god will decide. both goalies great at pens
